Nuxt.js 2.x Hands-On Handbook (4) — How Nuxt.js Works
This chapter is a quick chat about how Nuxt.js actually works.
Nuxt.js has two environments under the hood:
in your terminal and you'll see this output:
Why does this matter? When you're developing, you install a lot of third-party packages —
, etc. Knowing the two environments helps you avoid a really common error:
Fixing 'window or document undefined' in Nuxt
Nuxt.js runs both modes simultaneously, so if a package you install doesn't support Node.js, you hit that error:
The usual fix is one of these:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

How do you avoid hitting this in the first place? Honestly, it's experience. For example, Bootstrap stuff is only ever used on the client — you'd never use it in Node.js. Axios on the other hand can run in Node.js, so you don't need
there. The simplest tell is "try running it". Or you can read the package source and see if it uses browser-only APIs like
For now just know Nuxt.js has two environments — we'll come back to the differences in later sections.
